title: Schedule
text:
Week
Workshop
Core question




1
Distributions and summarising data
What does a variable actually look like?


2
Inference
When is a difference real?


3
Relationships between variables
How do two variables move together — and what does that tell us?


4
Regression I
What does a coefficient actually measure?


5
Regression II
What does “controlling for” actually mean?


6
Choosing your model
When is linear regression the wrong tool?


7
Understanding your model
Are you controlling for the right things?


8
Time and data
What goes wrong when observations are not independent?


9
Experiments and A/B testing
Why does randomisation solve the confounding problem?


10
When you can’t experiment
Can we estimate causal effects without randomisation?


11
Synthesis and assessment clinic
How do I choose a method — and what are its limits?



Weekly materials will be added here as the module progresses.

title: Week 1 — Reaction Time Exercise
text:
A regional bank is reviewing performance data from its customer service centre.
      Response time — how quickly agents react to incoming alerts — is a key operational metric.
      HR currently records a single test score per agent during onboarding.
      Your task: collect your own response time data and evaluate whether
      a single measurement is an adequate way to capture this metric.
    
    Your name
    
    Device you are using
    
      Select…
      Phone
      Laptop
    
    Had caffeine in the last 2 hours?
    
      Select…
      Yes
      No
    
    Index finger length (mm) — tip to base crease, on your dominant hand
    
    Use a ruler. This is for a later session — not related to your response time.
    Continue →
    Please enter your name, finger length, and answer all questions.
  

  
  
    How it works
    
      You will complete 10 trials.
      The screen will turn green after a random delay.
      Tap anywhere as fast as you can.
      Note down each time as it appears — you'll need them at the end.
    
    Start trials
  

  
  
    Trial 1 of 10
    Wait…
    Tap when the screen turns green
    Too early — wait for green!
  

  
  
    Trial 1 of 10
    TAP!
  

  
  
    All done!
    
    Saving your results…

title: Research Methods in Analytics
text:
This is the module page for 7002LBSAI Research Methods in Analytics, part of the MSc Business Analytics and AI programme at Liverpool John Moores University.
This module is the statistical engine room of the MSc. It bridges from descriptive analytics to causal inference, and feeds directly into Predictive Analytics and Machine Learning.

title: Research Methods in Analytics
text:
This is the module page for 7002LBSAI Research Methods in Analytics, part of the MSc Business Analytics and AI programme at Liverpool John Moores University.
This module is the statistical engine room of the MSc. It bridges from descriptive analytics to causal inference, and feeds directly into Predictive Analytics and Machine Learning.

title: Research Methods in Analytics
text:
Module overview



Module code
7002LBSAI


Credits
20


Delivery
11 × 3-hour workshops + 11 × 1-hour online sessions


Assessment
40% examination, 60% applied project


Start
September 2026

title: Research Methods in Analytics
text:
Module leader
Dr Steve Nolan Liverpool Business School, LJMU S.P.Nolan@ljmu.ac.uk

title: Resources
text:
Békés, G. & Kézdi, G. (2021). Data Analysis for Business, Economics, and Policy. Cambridge University Press.
Free materials and data at gabors-data-analysis.com.

title: Resources
text:
Software
This module uses Python for applied work, coordinated with the Programming for Business Analytics module.
Key packages: statsmodels, pandas, matplotlib, marginaleffects.
